steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Persad and colleagues’ conclusion has two parts: (1) concentrated precipitation triggers more irrigation, and (2) the size of that irrigation increase is highly sensitive to an area’s baseline precipitation concentration. The correct choice must show data from both baseline scenarios — somewhat concentrated and evenly distributed — so the contrast in irrigation increase is visible.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — compares irrigation increases in one scenario to aquifer changes in another: Mixes irrigation data from the concentrated-baseline scenario with aquifer data from the even-baseline scenario. The conclusion is about irrigation sensitivity, not aquifer changes, so this comparison is off-target. ✗
  • Choice B — concentrated baseline: small irrigation increase; even baseline: +9.0% surface, +7.9% groundwater: Presents irrigation data from both baseline scenarios side by side, showing a large contrast that directly illustrates how sensitive the irrigation increase is to the starting baseline. ✓
  • Choice C — concentrated baseline: aquifer +4.9%, irrigation +0.4% surface / +0.9% groundwater: Shows only the concentrated-baseline scenario and includes aquifer data the conclusion does not address. The contrast between baseline scenarios needed to demonstrate sensitivity is absent. ✗
  • Choice D — states irrigation “will decline” in the concentrated-baseline scenario: Contradicts the table, which shows only positive (increasing) values for irrigation. No data support a decline. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
